comments.author: @SnowWhite717_
comments.text: I lived remote in Alaska. Living in a remote community anywhere on earth has its hardships but they also have many rewards. Things money can’t buy. Now being older I had to move to a city with access to good healthcare but I still miss my home where the mountains met the sea. ❤ It will always be in my heart. Living remotely doesn’t mean living with less.

comments.author: @ComplexAdaptiveSystem
comments.text:
During the mid-20th century, the absolute isolation of the Line Islands made them ground zero for some of the most powerful explosions in human history.

Operation Grapple (United Kingdom)
In the late 1950s, Great Britain was desperate to solidify its status as a nuclear superpower by developing a thermonuclear weapon (hydrogen bomb). They chose Kiritimati (Christmas Island) and Malden Island as their testing grounds.

Between 1957 and 1958, the British military detonated a series of atmospheric nuclear weapons just off the coast or high above the atolls.

These tests successfully allowed the UK to join the elite "H-bomb club," forever altering the history of these remote coral islands.

Operation Dominic (United States)
A few years later, in 1962, the geopolitical landscape heated up again when the Soviet Union ended a temporary moratorium on nuclear testing. The United States responded rapidly with Operation Dominic, the largest atmospheric nuclear testing series ever conducted by the US.

Centered primarily around Kiritimati and the waters near Johnston Atoll, the US dropped and detonated 24 nuclear weapons in the atmosphere over a span of just a few months.

These tests included everything from massive air-dropped bombs to high-altitude space bursts launched via rockets.

The legacy of those frantic Cold War years left a deep mark on the region, involving thousands of military personnel and impacting the local populations. Today, the islands have returned to a quiet, isolated existence, but for a brief window in the 20th century, they were arguably the most explosive places on the planet.

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: Kiribati reminds me that being far away doesn't mean being far behind. Sometimes the places farthest from the world's noise hear life's important lessons the clearest. Meanwhile, I complain when the grocery store is ten minutes away. Could you imagine living somewhere this remote?

replies.author: @RoamPlanet-4K
replies.text: The limestone island of Banaba looks like an entirely different planet compared to the rest of Kiribati, but its history of phosphate mining is so heartbreaking. Was traveling to such a remote and sparsely populated place like Banaba much more difficult than visiting Tarawa? How long did it take you to actually reach the island?

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: That contrast hit me too. We often associate "remote" with "isolated," but places like Kiribati show a different kind of wealth—strong communities, deep traditions, and a connection to nature that many modern cities have lost. I honestly wonder how our priorities would change if the ocean, not the internet, was our main link to the world. What do you think you’d miss the most? 🌊🏝
